title: Aiptasia spawning and embryo and larvae handling - Pringle lab
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"6o5l8","text":"This protocol seeks to provide all information needed to allow regular spawning of Aiptasia and safe handling of the spawn. This fork contains improvements and more details compared to the original version.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1fqkk","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"53ve0","text":"It is based on the protocol described in Grawunder et al., 2015.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"7od9h","text":"The artificial seawater (ASW) used in the Pringle lab is Coral Pro Salt (manufactured by Red Sea) dissolved in de-ionized water to a salinity of between 32 and 34 g/L and filtered through 5-µm, 1-µm and 0.35-µm pore sizes.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f05ol","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1broe","text":"The Pringle lab uses the male, clonal Aiptasia strain CC7 to obtain sperm and the clonal female strain PLF3/F003. The female strains H2, PLF5 and PLF8 have been used successfully in the past. All strain combinations yield viable planula larvae. In addition, recently spawning could also be induced in dozens of wild-collected Aiptasia from Hawai'í and Florida (the reason for failure to spawn most commonly was that animals were not growing to the required sizes and/or had impaired health).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"80bf3","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"83n9l","text":"Strains (and therefore sexes) are kept separated to allow for full control over fertilization. Gametes are collected and eggs fertilized with a reasonable amount of sperm within 5-30 min of their release.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"d4Ezu","text":"Please see also the protocol on general Aiptasia husbandry for additional materials.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":84}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":20,"length":38}],"data":{}},{"key":"4falo","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"duvd6","text":"INCUBATORS:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4nge2","text":"FULL-MOON INCUBATOR","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3a0l0","text":"Percival E41 or AL41 light- and temperature-controlled incubator (only one shelf needed):","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":9,"length":3},{"key":2,"offset":16,"length":4}],"data":{}},{"key":"ct5it","text":"29°C temperature.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8dd3u","text":"20-30 µmol/m²/s white light from either fluorescent tubes (4100 K, CRI=80 Philips F25T8/TL841) or  SciWhite LEDs (4000 K, CRI=90). ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dp3hk","text":"Set to a 12 h light : 12 h dark regimen with the light phase from 4:30 PM to 4:30 AM. ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1nc9t","text":"Blue-light LEDs ~450 nm (or different combinations between 400 and 460 nm, the largest part of the spectrum should always be around 450 nm) with a photon flux of ~15-20 µmol/m²/s (direct read from PAR meter, not corrected for discrete blue wavelengths)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"align-left","offset":0,"length":252}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2hdr4","text":"     - We are using the following, discontinued LED bars:  Econlux SolarStinger DeepBlue LED bars with 400, 420, 440 and 460 nm wavelength","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"align-left","offset":0,"length":138},{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":138}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6ptob","text":"     - We have used single-wavelength ~450-nm LEDs (usually marketed as \"Actinic\" or \"Royal\" or sometimes \"Deep\" blue) without problems in the past:      Current USA TrueLumen LED Lunar Lights - 453nm Actinic Blue","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"align-left","offset":0,"length":213},{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":213}],"entityRanges":[{"key":3,"offset":154,"length":59}],"data":{}},{"key":"1egtf","text":"     - This LED bar might be a more solid choice and allows dimming: ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":69}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3l4rv","text":"Actinic Blue ReefBar Aquarium LED Light 9W/Ft - Reef Aquarium LED Bar","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":49}],"entityRanges":[{"key":5,"offset":0,"length":49}],"data":{}},{"key":"6n3tv","text":"Blue light on a weekly timer for Tue-Sat, 4:30 AM-4:30 PM.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"93bna","text":"     - The Percival incubators have internal outlets which in some models can be set on a schedule in the control interface of the incubator, otherwise a 7-day programmable timer power strip does a good job, e.g., ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":0,"length":214}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c83ov","text":"GE 7-Day Programmable Power Strip with Digital Timer - ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bq4ua","text":"Set to a normal 12 h light : 12 h dark regimen with the light phase from 6 AM to 6 PM. ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"92n08","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"49hl3","text":"EQUIPMENT:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":10}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"872ud","text":"Apogee MQ-200 or MQ-500 PAR meter","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":12,"offset":7,"length":16}],"data":{}},{"key":"77hel","text":"Onset HOBO loggers (to track light and temperature regimens on each shelf): ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"acpcr","text":"     - LEGACY: Onset HOBO UA-002-64 (requires extra optical read-out device and HOBOware app for macOS and Windows)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":13,"offset":15,"length":20},{"key":14,"offset":80,"length":8}],"data":{}},{"key":"21aar","text":"     - PREFERRED: Onset HOBO MX2202 (set-up and read-out via Bluetooth and HOBOconnect app for iOS, Android and Windows)","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":15,"offset":18,"length":17},{"key":16,"offset":75,"length":11}],"data":{}},{"key":"2gurr","text":"Stereomicroscope to observe embryonic development","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1c81l","text":"OPTIONAL: 100-150 W ceramic infra-red heat lamps (for ensuring a work-surface temperature of 25-30°C), can be found as supplies for reptile terraria, even available with a coupled thermostat, required distance from surface needs to be tested, for us ~60 cm led to a surface temperature of 27°C (100 W) or 30°C (150 W)","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":17,"offset":150,"length":40}],"data":{}},{"key":"aoq9r","text":"Red bike tail lights, e.g., 2-set from Amazon","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":22,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[{"key":18,"offset":28,"length":17}],"data":{}},{"key":"ahj3i","text":"We use the following nylon meshes from Pentair Aquatic Eco-Systems:","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":19,"offset":39,"length":27}],"data":{}},{"key":"ccrt8","text":"     - 20-µm nylon mesh, 3-20/14","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7vuvd","text":"     - 41-µm nylon mesh, M46","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cjpoj","text":"     - 125-µm nylon mesh, M125","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"77mf6","text":"     - 150-µm nylon mesh, M150","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8ijpd","text":"The meshes can be attached with hot glue to the previously removed bottom of a plastic 250-500 mL beaker. We use 3D-printed funnels with an exchangeable mesh-attachment system which can be stacked and are hold in position within the glass beakers. It is only one 150-µm sieve necessary for both eggs and larvae. steps: Collect pedal lacerates from the respective Aiptasia strain donor tank and transfer them into a medium tank with 500 mL ASW. Use ~50 pedal lacerates per tank. Replenish pedal lacerate numbers in these tanks continuously from the donor tanks.
Transfer fully developed small anemones into a large tank with 1.5 L ASW. This is the 'tiny polyp' tank. a

To grow animals to a good spawning size numbers of animals will be reduced step-by-step as the anemones grow. All preparation steps can be done at room temperature (22-27°C) under cool-white light (~4000 K) with ~25 µmol photons/m 2 /s in clear tanks of the indicated sizes. Growing the animals in incubators at 27-29°C increases growth rates slightly. The list with sizes and models of containers used in this protocol can be found in the Materials section. Feed anemones in all tanks twice a week and do water changes 4-8 h later. Remove excess biofilm and pedal lacerates with disposable/clean spatulas. Inside walls can be wiped clean with Kimwipes but that is usually not necessary and increases risk of contamination. Depending on temperature and feeding regimen, growth from a pedal lacerate to a spawning-sized animal takes 3-5 months. This protocol is optimized for obtaining a constant number of spawning-sized animals for four ten-tank sets of spawners with each 6-7 tanks of females (PLF3) and 3-4 tanks of males (CC7), while keeping the workload for maintanance at manageable levels. If the spawning animals are needed faster, a higher feeding (and therefore cleaning) frequency and low numbers of anemones at all sizes will accelerate growth.
Transfer polyps with a pedal disc diameter of 1-3 mm into a new large tank with 1.5 L ASW. This is the 'small polyps' tank. a
Transfer polyps with a pedal disc diameter of 3-5 mm into a new large tank with 1.5 L ASW. This is the 'medium polyps' tank. a
Transfer anemones with a pedal disc diameter of 5-6 mm into a medium tank with 500 mL ASW and maximal 10 anemones per tank. This is the 'large polyps' tank. a

When polyps from the 'large polyp' tank reach a pedal disc diameter >8 mm transfer 5 animals of the same strain (and therefore sex) into a small tank with 200 mL ASW. This tank is ready to be transferred into the spawning incubator. a These animals will start spawning sporadically, but for better success and reliability grow polyps to 10-15 mm pedal disc size.

We simulate a simple moon cycle over 28 days to induce spawning: Animals are subjected to five nights of "full moon" followed by 23 dark nights. We use two Percival incubators for spawning. Both are set to a temperature of 29°C and an inverted 12 h light : 12 h dark regimen (light from 4:30 PM to 4:30 AM, dark from 4:30 AM to 4:30 PM). Light flux is ~25 µmol photons/m²/s for day light (cool white at ~4000 K). The first, "full-moon", incubator with one used shelf has set up five nights (4:30 AM to 4:30 PM; Tue-Sat) of blue light (Econlux SolarStinger DeepBlue LED bars with 400, 420, 440 and 460 nm wavelength; discontinued, see Materials for alternatives) with a photon flux of ~15-20 µmol/m²/s followed by two dark nights (Sun-Mon). The second, "new moon" incubator with three used shelves does not have blue light set up. The inverted day-night cycle allows to obtain spawning between ~9 AM and ~11 AM. Spawning usually occurs between 5-7 h after onset of darkness. We maintain four sets of spawners. Each set consists of ten tanks, 6-7 tanks with females and 3-4 tanks of males and is kept on a large tray for easy handling. Each tray occupies an individual shelf, one tray in the full-moon incubator, the other three in the new-moon incubator. Each Monday evening, another set is moved to the full-moon incubator so they are all offset by one week in their moon cycle to get get regular weekly spawning. Usually, eggs are the limiting factor, therefore the higher number of female compared to male tanks in each spawning set, but this composition can be adjusted if sperm becomes limiting.
Add new spawning tanks from step 8 to a spawning set seven days before full moon starts to acclimate. Each spawning tank is subjected to 3-6 moon cycles and then replaced.

Feed all tanks in the spawning incubators at least four times per week with saturating amounts of living brine shrimp nauplii in the morning after the last spawn check. Change water and clean tanks from biofilm and all pedal lacerates with disposable spatulas in the afternoon after onset of 'daylight' in the incubators. Also do a water change on Mondays and clean especially the long sides and bottom of the tanks from the sets expected to spawn that week to make it easier to spot gametes. Usually thorough cleaning of the tanks is only necessary once a week, for the other days, a good rinse from a squirt bottle of ASW and water change is sufficient. If tanks get too dirty, do a full tank change but only do that every couple of months as too frequent changes stress the anemones and reduce spawning efficiency. Do tank changes on Fridays, to allow anemones to securely attach over the weekend.

The spawning animals are very sensitive to 'out-of-cycle' light cues. Therefore check for spawning in the dark with a red light 5-7 h (~9 AM to ~11 AM for our light regimen) after onset of the dark phase. Usually, the animals spawn 10-13 days after end of the full-moon phase. Although, they sometimes spawn later during the cycle. Therefore, checking all incubators during on working days helps to not miss any spawning event. In the spawning time window, check every 30 min. Our full-moon phase is set to Tuesday to Saturday and spawning happens mostly from Tuesdays (usually the best spawns) to Thursdays sometimes on Mondays or Fridays. Aiptasia eggs are white, round and about 80 µm in diameter. They are visible by bare eyes if released to the water and sometimes sink and form patches on the ground. If an animal is still in the process of releasing eggs when checking, put it aside in the incubator and take 5-10 min later. a Spermatozoa are much smaller (the head is 3-3.5 µm long and around 1.5 µm thick, the tails is about 50 µm long) more difficult to see. With a good amount of sperm, the water in the tank should be decently turbid up to looking almost milky. The example tank shown below has the absolute minimum density to use for successful fertilizations, with five big males, it should be immediately clear whether the males in the tank spawned or not. Sufficient sperm density and quality are the most critical points for reliable fertilizations. a OPTIONAL: To confirm sperm in the water, a sample might be viewed on a standard light microscope with a total magnification of 600× (60× objective and 10× eye pieces) or higher (the example microscope image actually shows concentrated sperm; see below). A way to ensure that the sample doesn't dry out to fast and the spermatozoa don't get squashed is to use PTFE-coated glass slides with multiple wells as often used for immunofluorescence (the slide can be cleaned with DI water and then ethanol and reused for checking on sperm). Another advantage of these slides is that the initial focus plane can be adjusted at the borders of the wells. Once a the focal plane is adjusted it might take some time to find spermatozoa, it might help to change the focus slightly during the search to find a tail and then follow it to the head. The density of the sperm in the water is usually very low (even if it looks very milky by bare eye). To make observation of sperm easier centrifugation of 1000 µL of the sperm-containing water in a 1.5-mL microcentrifuge tube for 5 min at 4000 ×g can help. Pour out most of the liquid afterwards and resuspend the sperm in the remaining 10-50 µL of ASW.

Decant the egg-containing water into two of the 250-mL glass dishes through a 150-µm nylon mesh to remove any large chunks of debris and dirt and then rinse the mesh immediately with a small amount of ASW to recover any eggs sticking to the mesh. If eggs are found and settled to patches on the ground of the tank, agitate the tank carefully before decanting. Don't use polystyrene for handling the unfertilized eggs as this causes damage to them.

Pour the sperm-containig water through two stacked sieves (125-µm and 20-µm nylon mesh) to remove larger debris particles. Collect the sperm in a 1-L glass beaker reserved for this purpose only. If multiple male tanks (with the same strain) spawned, combine the sperm. If the sperm density is vastly different between the tanks, use only those with high densities.

To fertilize the eggs with sperm, use a disposable wide-mouth polyethylene transfer pipette to add five or more pipette volumes (~20-25 mL) of the filtered sperm to each large glass dish with ~100 mL of eggs. Fertilized eggs can be concentrated by gentle swirling in the center of the large glass dishes and then distributed with a large-mouth transfer pipette into 60×15-mm glass petri dishes if needed. Again, don't use polystyrene as it will reduce fertilization and viability.

If the fertilization is successful, embryos in the 4-cell stage should be observable under a routine stereomicroscope within 1 h after fertilization. The lower the temperature, the slower are the divisions. Ciliated, motile blastulae are usually observable 4-6 h after fertilization.

Set up pedal lacerate donor tanks of each strain. Use large tanks with 20-40 large animals (pedal disc diameter >8 mm). These will be the "parents" of all pedal lacerates and subsequent spawning animals. For redundancy, set up two tanks per strain. These tanks can be kept at room temperature (22-27°C) under ~25 µmol photons/m 2 /s of cool white light (~4000 K) and fed and cleaned once per week. If increased pedal lacerate production is required, they can be fed multiple times per week and kept in incubators at 27-29°C. Pedal lacerates should be removed at every water change and either transferred into the respective tank from the next step or discarded. The reason for using these extra tanks and not just to collect pedal lacerates from the future-spawner tanks is to keep the parents to some extend consistent and independent to be able to start completely new tanks in case of die-offs/diseases in the future spawner tanks.

To minimize out-of-cycle light exposure of the animals, use a light-proof transport container to move the spawned tanks to the working area and turn off ceiling lights when collecting the gametes from the tanks.
Add ~200 mL fresh ASW to the tank and place back in the light-proof transport container until finishing with fertilizations, then return the tanks to their respective incubator shelves.
Add ~200 mL fresh ASW to the tank and place back in the light-proof transport container until finishing with fertilizations, then return the tanks to their respective incubator shelves.
1 d after fertilization - water change:

Place the fertilized eggs into an incubator or use an IR-heating lamp to keep them between 25°C and 30°C, IF EXPOSED TO TEMPERATURES <25°C EVEN FOR ASHORT TIME, MOST EMBRYOS WILL NOT DEVELOP AND DISINTEGRATE OVERNIGHT.

Check health and development of embryos/larvae under a stereo-microscope. The larvae are almost fully developed after ~24 h with a mouth, gastric cavity and clearly distinct tissue layers, only the apical tuft usually takes 36-48 h to form, Depending on their mood, in the best case, the larvae should be swimming around in the dish showing some light attraction, but some batches just sit on the bottom of the dish with little motion, that isn't necessarily a bad sign and can change within minutes.
Carefully swirl the dish with the larvae to detach any larvae from the internal surfaces. Do not swirl too violently as that frees up a lot of the debris stuck to the bottom.
Pre-wet the mesh of the 150-µm sieve with some ASW and stack it on top of the 41-µm sieve.
Fill a 2000-mL glass beaker with ~1200 mL ASW. And place the 41-µm sieve inside. The mesh at the bottom of the sieve should be just immersed in the ASW.
Remove the 150-µm sieve from the assembly and put it aside.
Take a new 250-mL glass dish, cover just the bottom with ASW and transfer the label from the old dish.
Pour the water with the larvae into the stacked sieves. The pouring from those glass dishes is pretty messy, be careful not to spill too much of the water.
Rinse the 150-µm sieve to detach any larvae stuck to it and get them into the 41-µm sieve below.
Gently rinse down the dish with some ASW and pour that into the sieves as well.

IMMEDIATELY rinse the mesh with ASW to move the larvae into the ASW of the dish. Rinse in circular movements from the edges towards the center of the mesh. Larvae should be visible on the mesh, make sure all of them got transferred during rinsing.
Lift the 41-µm sieve slowly out of the water, and quickly place it inverted on top of the new dish.
Swirl and move the 41-µm strainer several times slowly up and down for removal of most dirt/ciliates. ALWAYS KEEP THE MESH SUBMERGED IN THE ASW!

Rinse sieves with DI water followed by ASW if more larvae need to get water changes. If not, rinse only with DI water, then spray the insides and meshes with 70% ethanol and after a few seconds rinse again with DI water and place upside-down on a clean surface for drying.

Empty the 2000-mL beaker and rinse thoroughly with DI water. If more larvae need water changes, repeat the previous steps, if not, spray the insides with 70% ethanol, wait 30 s and rinse several times thoroughly with DI water and place upside-down on a clean surface for drying.
Cover the dishes with the cleaned larvae with clear lids and place back in the incubator.
Once per week (or as needed):

Perform the water change procedure again. The larvae will shrink over time (if unfed) and start to disintegrate between 20-30 d after fertilization. Use a 20-µm sieve instead of the 41-µm one from two weeks after fertilization onwards to not loose the shrunken larvae or transfer them manually with a micropipette into a new dish with fresh ASW to prevent loss completely.
creator: Christian CR Renicke
